3 min read
Just now

--

For more than a decade, marketplaces like ThemeForest helped thousands of WordPress businesses reach customers around the world. They provided visibility, trust, and a simple way to distribute themes and plugins.
Many successful WordPress companies — including Reacthemes — grew through this ecosystem.
But as products mature and businesses evolve, the marketplace model starts to reveal its limitations. Today, an increasing number of WordPress companies are investing in direct sales through their own websites instead of relying solely on third-party marketplaces.
This isn’t just a pricing decision — it’s a long-term product and customer strategy.
The Marketplace Model Was Built for Discovery
Marketplaces are excellent for helping customers discover products. They handle payments, expose products to a large audience, and provide a familiar buying experience.
For many companies, they’re the perfect place to launch.
However, as products gain traction, companies often realize they need greater control over their customer experience and business growth.
The Challenges of Marketplace Dependency
One of the biggest challenges is revenue sharing. Marketplace commissions reduce the resources available for development, support, documentation, security improvements, and innovation.
But revenue isn’t the only factor.
Selling through a marketplace also creates distance between the product team and the customer. Every update, communication, and support interaction depends on another platform’s workflow.
For engineering teams shipping frequent improvements, that can slow down innovation.
Why Direct Sales Make Sense
Selling directly allows a company to own the complete customer journey — from purchase to onboarding, updates, renewals, and long-term support.
That creates several advantages:
Faster Product Updates
Security patches, bug fixes, and new features can be delivered immediately without waiting for marketplace review processes.
Better Customer Support
Instead of working through marketplace limitations, customers communicate directly with the team building the product.
This often leads to faster responses and better support experiences.
Greater Investment in the Product
When more revenue stays with the company, it can be reinvested into:

• Product development

• Security improvements

• Accessibility

• Documentation

• Customer support

• Performance optimization
Ultimately, customers benefit from a better product.
A Better Experience for Customers
Buying directly isn’t simply about changing where a payment happens.
It often means:

• Longer support periods

• Simpler renewals

• Faster access to updates

• Direct communication with the development team

• A smoother overall experience
For customers, the product remains the same — but the experience surrounding it improves.
What This Means for Engineering Teams
As a software engineer, I find this transition particularly interesting.
When a company owns its distribution platform, engineering work has a more direct impact on the business.
Improving security, accessibility, performance, and user experience doesn’t just make the software better — it also strengthens customer satisfaction, retention, and long-term growth.
Every release becomes an investment in both the product and the relationship with its users.
Looking Ahead
Marketplaces will continue to play an important role in the WordPress ecosystem, especially for discovery.
But for established product companies, direct sales offer something marketplaces cannot: complete ownership of the customer experience.
That’s why we’re seeing more companies invest in their own platforms, communities, documentation, and support systems.
The future isn’t about abandoning marketplaces entirely.
It’s about building stronger relationships with customers — one direct connection at a time.
